title: My Family
text:
My name is Emma. I am twelve years old. I live in a small house in London.

I have a big family. My mother's name is Sarah. She is a teacher. My father's name is Tom. He is a doctor. I have one sister and one brother. My sister is eight years old. Her name is Lucy. My brother is fifteen years old. His name is Jack.

We have a dog. His name is Max. Max is very friendly. He is black and white.

I love my family very much.

questions.q: How old is Emma?
What is Emma's mother's job?
What is the dog's name?
What colour is the dog?

title: A Normal Day
text:
I wake up at seven o'clock every morning. I have breakfast in the kitchen. I eat bread and drink orange juice.

I go to school at eight o'clock. I walk to school. My school is near my house. I have classes from nine to three.

After school, I do my homework. Then I watch TV or read a book. I have dinner with my family at seven o'clock.

I go to bed at ten o'clock. I am happy with my day.

questions.q: What time does the writer wake up?
How does the writer go to school?
What does the writer do after school?
What time is dinner?

title: My Room
text:
My room is small but beautiful. The walls are blue. I love blue. There is a big window in my room. I can see the garden from my window.

I have a desk near the window. I do my homework at my desk. There are many books on my desk. I love reading.

My bed is next to the door. The bed is comfortable. I sleep very well.

There is a poster on the wall. It is a poster of my favourite football team. My room is my favourite place in the house.

questions.q: What colour are the walls?
Where is the desk?
What is on the wall?
What can the writer see from the window?

title: At the Market
text:
Every Saturday morning, Maria goes to the local market with her mother. The market is in the town centre and it opens at eight o'clock.

There are many stalls at the market. Maria's favourite stall sells fresh fruit and vegetables. The tomatoes are always very red and the oranges smell wonderful. Maria usually buys apples and bananas for the week.

There is also a bakery stall. The bread there is delicious. The baker makes it every morning before the market opens. Maria always buys one loaf of brown bread.

After shopping, Maria and her mother sit at a small café near the market. They drink coffee and talk about their plans for the weekend. Maria loves these Saturday mornings. They make her feel happy.

questions.q: When does Maria go to the market?
What does Maria usually buy from the fruit stall?
When does the baker make the bread?
What do Maria and her mother do after shopping?

title: London: A Great City
text:
London is the capital city of England. About nine million people live there. It is one of the most visited cities in the world.

London has many famous places. The Tower of London is very old. It is over 900 years old. Buckingham Palace is where the King lives. Thousands of tourists visit it every day.

The city has excellent public transport. You can travel by underground train, called 'the Tube'. It is fast and easy. There are also red double-decker buses. They are very famous.

London has many parks too. Hyde Park is very large. People walk, run and relax there. In summer, the parks are full of people enjoying the sunshine.

If you visit London, you should try fish and chips. It is the traditional English food. Shops that sell it are called 'chippies'.

questions.q: How many people live in London?
What is the underground train in London called?
What is the traditional English food mentioned in the text?
What is Hyde Park used for?

title: The Job Interview
text:
Carlos was very nervous. He had a job interview at nine o'clock. The job was at a big hotel in the city centre.

He woke up early and put on his best clothes. He checked his CV again and again. He wanted to work as a receptionist because he loved talking to people.

At the hotel, a woman called Ms Parker met him. She was very friendly. She asked him many questions. 'Why do you want this job?' she asked. 'I love helping people,' Carlos answered. 'And I speak three languages.'

Ms Parker smiled. 'That's very useful,' she said. 'We have guests from many countries.'

Two days later, Carlos received an email. He got the job! He was so happy that he called his mother immediately.

questions.q: Why was Carlos nervous?
What job did Carlos want?
How many languages does Carlos speak?
How did Carlos find out he got the job?
opts: He went back to the hotel

title: Social Media: Good or Bad?
titleTr: Sosyal Medya: İyi mi, Kötü mü?
text:
Social media is part of everyday life for millions of people. Platforms like Instagram, TikTok and X (formerly Twitter) allow users to share photos, videos and opinions instantly. But is this a good thing?

There are clear benefits. Social media helps people stay connected with friends and family, even when they live far apart. It also gives people a voice — ordinary citizens can share news and raise awareness about important issues. Many small businesses have grown significantly by using social media to reach new customers.

However, there are serious concerns. Research suggests that heavy social media use is linked to anxiety and low self-esteem, particularly among teenagers. People often compare their real lives to the carefully edited highlights of others, which can lead to feelings of inadequacy.

Another problem is the spread of false information. Stories and images can be shared thousands of times before anyone checks whether they are true.

Despite these issues, most experts agree that social media itself is not the problem. It depends on how we use it. Setting time limits, following positive accounts and taking regular breaks can make social media a much healthier experience.

questions.q: According to the text, what is one benefit of social media for businesses?
opts: It helps them reach new customers

questions.q: What does research suggest about heavy social media use in teenagers?
opts: It is linked to anxiety and low self-esteem
It makes them more social in person

questions.q: What is described as another problem with social media?
What do most experts say about social media?
opts: It should be banned for under-18s
How we use it is what matters

title: The New Neighbour
text:
When Maya moved to her new flat in Bristol, she did not know anyone in the building. She had moved from a small village where everybody knew everybody else. City life felt cold and anonymous.

For the first few weeks, she barely left her flat. She worked from home, ordered food online and tried to ignore the loneliness growing inside her.

One Tuesday morning, the lift broke. Maya had to carry her groceries up four flights of stairs. On the third floor, she bumped into an elderly man struggling with a heavy bag.

'Let me help you,' she said, without thinking.

The man looked surprised. 'Thank you. People don't usually offer,' he said with a smile. His name was Mr Okafor. He had lived in the building for fifteen years.

They talked all the way to the fourth floor. He told her about the neighbourhood — which café had the best coffee, which park was quiet on weekday mornings. Before saying goodbye, he invited her for tea the following Sunday.

That one broken lift changed everything. Within a month, Maya had been introduced to six of her neighbours. The city no longer felt cold. It just needed a little patience.

questions.q: Why did Maya feel lonely at first?
opts: She did not like her flat
She did not know anyone and city life felt cold

questions.q: How did Maya meet Mr Okafor?
opts: When the lift broke and she helped him

questions.q: What did Mr Okafor tell Maya about?
What is the main message of the story?
opts: Cities are too dangerous to live in
Small acts of kindness can change things

title: Climate Change and Young People
text:
Around the world, young people are becoming increasingly concerned about climate change. Many feel that governments and large corporations are not acting fast enough to protect the planet.

In recent years, student protests have taken place in dozens of countries. Inspired by activists like Greta Thunberg, millions of students have left classrooms to demand action. They argue that it is their future — and the future of generations not yet born — that is at risk.

Scientists agree that the situation is serious. Global temperatures have risen by about 1.1 degrees Celsius since the industrial revolution. If this continues, extreme weather events — including floods, droughts and heatwaves — will become more frequent and more severe.

However, some progress is being made. Renewable energy sources such as solar and wind power are growing rapidly. Many countries have committed to reaching 'net zero' carbon emissions by 2050.

Young people are not just protesting — they are also making changes in their own lives. Many are reducing meat consumption, travelling less by plane and choosing second-hand clothing. Small individual choices, combined with large-scale political action, may be what saves us.

questions.q: What have millions of students done to demand climate action?
By how much have global temperatures risen since the industrial revolution?
What is the target many countries have set for carbon emissions?
According to the text, what personal changes are some young people making?
opts: Using electric cars and solar panels

title: The Science of Sleep
text:
For much of human history, sleep was considered little more than a passive state — a period of unconsciousness that the body required to rest. Modern neuroscience has fundamentally changed that view. Sleep, it turns out, is an extraordinarily active process during which the brain performs critical maintenance work.

During sleep, the brain consolidates memories, transferring information from short-term to long-term storage. This is why students who sleep well after studying tend to retain information significantly better than those who stay up all night. The brain also uses sleep to clear away metabolic waste products that accumulate during waking hours — a process that some researchers believe may be connected to the development of Alzheimer's disease when it goes wrong.

Sleep deprivation has consequences far beyond simple tiredness. After just 24 hours without sleep, cognitive performance deteriorates to a level comparable with being legally drunk. Chronic sleep deprivation is associated with increased risk of obesity, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease and depression.

Despite this, modern life seems almost designed to prevent adequate sleep. Artificial light — particularly the blue light emitted by screens — suppresses the production of melatonin, the hormone that signals to the body that it is time to sleep. Social pressures, work demands and the always-on nature of digital communication mean that many people treat sleep as a luxury rather than a biological necessity.

Experts recommend seven to nine hours of sleep per night for adults, yet surveys consistently show that large proportions of the population fall significantly short of this target.

questions.q: According to the text, what does the brain do during sleep regarding memories?
opts: It consolidates memories from short-term to long-term storage
It creates new memories from imagination

questions.q: What does the text suggest about 24 hours without sleep?
opts: It is harmless for most people
It impairs cognition similarly to being drunk

questions.q: Why does blue light from screens affect sleep?
What is the main argument the text makes about modern life and sleep?
opts: Technology has helped people sleep better
Modern life is structured in a way that works against good sleep
People simply choose not to sleep enough
Sleep problems only affect working adults

title: The Perfect Candidate
text:
Eleanor had prepared for this interview for three weeks. She had researched the company obsessively, rehearsed every possible question and bought a new suit specifically for the occasion. She was, she told herself, the perfect candidate.

The interview panel consisted of three people. The woman in the centre, whose name badge read 'Dr Chen', barely looked up when Eleanor entered. The man on the left, Mr Walsh, had a warm smile that Eleanor immediately trusted. The third interviewer was young — shockingly young, Eleanor thought — and kept taking notes on a tablet.

The first questions were straightforward. Eleanor answered confidently, drawing on her fifteen years of experience. She could see Mr Walsh nodding.

Then Dr Chen looked up. 'Tell me about a time you failed,' she said flatly.

Eleanor had prepared this answer too. She talked about a project that had gone over budget, how she had managed the situation, what she had learned. She thought it went well.

She did not get the job. The rejection email was polite and said nothing useful. For days, Eleanor replayed the interview, certain that Dr Chen had already decided against her before she had even sat down.

It did not occur to her, not once, that her answer about failure had contained no actual failure at all — only a story of competent crisis management dressed up as a mistake.

questions.q: How long had Eleanor been preparing for the interview?
Which interviewer did Eleanor immediately trust?
What did Eleanor believe was the reason she did not get the job?
opts: Dr Chen had decided against her before the interview began
Her answer about failure was too honest

questions.q: What is the real reason hinted at in the final paragraph?
opts: Eleanor was too experienced for the role
Her failure story wasn't really about failure at all

title: The Paradox of Urban Loneliness
text:
Cities are, by definition, places of density. Millions of people live within a few square kilometres, sharing streets, parks, transport networks and air. And yet, paradoxically, urban environments are also sites of profound loneliness. Studies consistently show that city dwellers report higher rates of social isolation than people in rural communities — despite being surrounded by far more people.

This phenomenon is sometimes called the 'urban loneliness paradox'. Researchers point to several contributing factors. First, urban life tends to be transient: people move frequently, making sustained relationships harder to build. Second, the design of many modern cities prioritises individual movement over communal gathering — cars over cafés, commutes over conversations.

There is also a psychological dimension. When surrounded by strangers, humans tend to withdraw rather than engage. The social psychologist Stanley Milgram described this as 'urban overload' — the brain's protective response to excessive social stimulation. We shut down in order to cope.

Some cities are beginning to take the problem seriously. Amsterdam has introduced 'loneliness benches' in parks — specially marked seats that signal to passers-by that the occupant welcomes conversation. Tokyo has established a Ministry of Loneliness. The United Kingdom appointed a Minister for Loneliness as far back as 2018.

Whether policy interventions can truly address what is fundamentally a structural and cultural problem remains to be seen. But the acknowledgement that loneliness is a public health issue — not merely a personal failing — represents genuine progress.

questions.q: What does the 'urban loneliness paradox' describe?
opts: The fact that rural people are lonelier than city people
City dwellers feeling lonely despite being surrounded by many people
The difficulty of making friends online
The isolation caused by modern technology

questions.q: According to Stanley Milgram, why do people withdraw in cities?
opts: They are too busy to socialise
The brain protects itself from excessive social stimulation
City people are naturally more introverted

questions.q: What is the purpose of 'loneliness benches' in Amsterdam?
opts: To give homeless people a place to sit
To mark historic locations in the city
To signal that the person sitting welcomes conversation
To provide rest areas for elderly residents

questions.q: What does the writer suggest about loneliness at the end of the text?
opts: It is entirely caused by social media
It is being recognised as a public health issue, which is progress
Policy alone can solve the problem
Only rural communities can escape loneliness

questions.title: My Family
questions.text:
My name is Emma. I am twelve years old. I live in a small house with my family.

My father is a teacher. His name is David. He teaches math at a school near our house. He wakes up early every morning and drinks coffee before he goes to work.

My mother is a nurse. Her name is Sarah. She works at a hospital. She helps sick people every day. She is very kind and patient.

I have one brother. His name is Tom. He is eight years old. Tom loves football. He plays football in the garden after school.

We also have a cat. Her name is Mia. She is white and small. Mia sleeps on the sofa all day. At night, she sits on my bed.

On weekends, we eat breakfast together. My mother makes eggs and toast. My father reads the newspaper. Tom and I watch cartoons. It is a happy time for our family.

I love my family very much.

questions.q: What does Emma's father do?
What is the name of the family's cat?
How old is Tom?
What does the family do on weekends?

questions.title: Colors Around Us
questions.text:
Colors are everywhere. We can see colors in the sky, in flowers, and in food.

The sky is blue during the day. At night, the sky is black. In the morning, the sky can be orange or pink. These colors are very beautiful.

Flowers have many colors. Roses are red or pink. Sunflowers are yellow. Violets are purple. Trees have green leaves in summer. In autumn, the leaves become orange, red, and yellow.

Food also has many colors. Apples are red or green. Bananas are yellow. Oranges are orange. Tomatoes are red. Grapes can be green or purple.

Colors can show feelings. Red means love or danger. Blue means calm. Yellow means happy. Green means nature.

What is your favorite color?

questions.q: What color is the sky at night?
What color are sunflowers?
In the text, what does yellow mean?
What color do tree leaves become in autumn?

questions.title: A Day at the Market
questions.text:
Every Saturday, Ben and his mother go to the market. The market is near their house. They walk there.

The market has many things. There are vegetables, fruits, bread, and cheese. There are also flowers and clothes.

Ben's mother buys tomatoes, potatoes, and onions. She also buys apples and oranges. Ben carries the bag. The bag is heavy!

Ben sees a toy shop next to the market. He stops and looks at a red toy car. It is very nice. He asks his mother, "Can I have this car?" His mother looks at the price. She says, "Not today, Ben. It is too expensive." Ben is a little sad.

Then his mother buys him an ice cream. It is chocolate flavor. Ben smiles. He is happy again.

They walk home. Ben eats his ice cream slowly. It is a good day.

questions.q: When do Ben and his mother go to the market?
Why doesn't Ben get the toy car?
What does his mother buy Ben instead?
How does Ben feel at the end?

questions.title: Animals at Home
questions.text:
Many people have animals at home. These animals are called pets. Pets are friendly animals that live with people.

Dogs are very popular pets. They are loyal and playful. Dogs like to walk and run. They eat meat and special dog food. A dog is often called "man's best friend."

Cats are also very popular. Cats sleep a lot — sometimes sixteen hours a day! They are quiet and clean. Cats like fish and special cat food. They also like to play with small toys.

Fish are easy pets. They live in a glass bowl or a tank. Fish need clean water and special food. They are beautiful to watch.

Birds can also be pets. Parrots are clever birds. Some parrots can talk! They repeat words and sounds.

All pets need food, water, and love. When you have a pet, you must take care of it every day.

questions.q: How many hours a day can cats sleep?
What makes parrots special?
opts: They are the most popular pet.

questions.q: What do all pets need according to the text?
What is a dog often called?

questions.title: The Lost Backpack
questions.text:
It was a normal Tuesday morning. Jake put on his shoes, took his lunch box, and ran to school. But when he arrived in class, he noticed something was wrong. His backpack was not there.

Jake checked under his desk and behind his chair. Nothing. He checked his locker in the corridor. Still nothing. His heart sank. Inside the backpack were his homework, his pencil case, and — most importantly — his favorite book.

He told his teacher, Mr. Collins. Mr. Collins made an announcement over the school speaker: "If anyone finds a blue backpack with a soccer ball keychain, please bring it to Room 14."

At lunch, Jake sat quietly and didn't eat much. His friend Lily sat next to him. "Don't worry," she said. "Somebody will find it."

After lunch, there was a knock on the classroom door. A younger student, maybe nine years old, stood at the door holding Jake's blue backpack. "I found this in the gym," the boy said. "I think it's yours."

Jake jumped up. He opened the bag — everything was there. The homework, the pencil case, and the book. He smiled widely and said thank you to the boy.

At the end of the day, Jake gave the boy a chocolate bar as a thank you. "You're very honest," Jake told him. The boy smiled and ran back to his class.

questions.q: Where did Jake look for his backpack first?
opts: Under his desk and in his locker

questions.q: What was the most important thing inside the backpack for Jake?
How did Mr. Collins try to help?
opts: He made an announcement over the speaker.

questions.q: What did Jake give the younger boy as a thank you?

questions.title: Why Do We Dream?
questions.text:
Almost everyone dreams. But why do we dream? Scientists are still not completely sure, but they have some interesting ideas.

When we sleep, our brain does not stop working. In fact, during a part of sleep called REM (Rapid Eye Movement), our brain is almost as active as when we are awake. This is when most dreams happen.

Some scientists think that dreams help us process our feelings. If something made you angry or worried during the day, you might dream about it at night. Your brain is trying to understand and organize those feelings.

Other scientists believe that dreams help us practice for the future. In dreams, we sometimes face difficult situations. This might help our brain prepare for real challenges.

Dreams can be happy, scary, or very strange. Many people dream about flying, falling, or being in a place they don't recognize. These are called "common dreams" because so many people have them.

Most people dream for about two hours every night. However, we usually forget most of our dreams shortly after we wake up. If you want to remember your dreams, try writing them down immediately after waking.

Dreaming is a natural and important part of sleep. Even animals dream — scientists have seen dogs and cats moving their legs while sleeping, probably because they are dreaming!

questions.q: What is REM sleep?
opts: A stage of sleep when most dreams happen
Deep sleep with no brain activity

questions.q: According to the text, why might you dream about something that made you angry?
opts: Because your body is too hot
Because your brain is trying to process your feelings
Because you are not sleeping well

questions.q: What does the text suggest to help remember your dreams?
opts: Write them down immediately after waking

questions.q: How do we know that animals also dream?
opts: They make sounds when they sleep.
They sometimes move their legs while sleeping.
They wake up suddenly at night.

questions.title: The New Neighbor
questions.text:
On a warm September afternoon, a big moving truck stopped in front of the empty house across the street. Sophie watched from her window with curiosity.

A family got out of a car behind the truck. There was a mother, a father, and a girl who looked about Sophie's age. The girl had dark, curly hair and wore a bright yellow jacket. She looked around nervously at the new neighborhood.

Sophie wanted to say hello, but she felt shy. "What if she doesn't like me?" she thought. She watched all afternoon as the family carried boxes into the house.

The next morning, Sophie saw the girl sitting alone on the front steps. She was reading a book. Sophie took a deep breath, picked up two glasses of lemonade from the kitchen, and walked across the street.

"Hi, I'm Sophie. I live over there," she said, pointing to her house. "I made some lemonade. Do you want some?"

The girl looked up and smiled. "Yes, please! I'm Amara. We just moved from Brazil."

They sat on the steps and talked for over an hour. Amara told Sophie about her old city, her school, and her love of football. Sophie told Amara about the neighborhood — the best park, the ice cream shop, and the friendly librarian.

By the end of the afternoon, they were already making plans for the weekend. Sophie felt happy. She had a new friend, and it was easier than she expected.

questions.q: Why didn't Sophie say hello to Amara immediately?
What did Sophie bring when she went to talk to Amara?
Where did Amara's family move from?
What did Sophie tell Amara about?
opts: The neighborhood — parks, shops, and the library

questions.title: The Ocean: Earth's Largest Habitat
questions.titleTr: Okyanus: Dünyanın En Büyük Yaşam Alanı
questions.text:
The ocean covers more than 70% of the Earth's surface. It is the largest habitat on our planet and is home to millions of different plants and animals.

The ocean is divided into several parts. The Pacific Ocean is the largest and the deepest. The Atlantic Ocean is the second largest. There is also the Indian Ocean, the Arctic Ocean, and the Southern Ocean.

The ocean has different zones. Near the surface, there is plenty of sunlight. Many fish, dolphins, and sea turtles live here. The deeper you go, the darker and colder it becomes. In the very deep parts, called the "deep sea," there is no sunlight at all. Strangely, many unusual creatures live there, such as the anglerfish, which creates its own light.

The ocean is very important for life on Earth. It produces more than half of the world's oxygen. It also controls the climate — ocean currents carry warm or cool water around the world, which affects the weather in many countries.

Unfortunately, the ocean is in danger. Plastic pollution is a serious problem. Every year, millions of tonnes of plastic enter the sea. Animals can eat this plastic and die. Many countries are now working to reduce plastic waste and clean up the ocean.

We all depend on the ocean for food, air, and climate. Protecting it is one of the most important jobs we have.

questions.q: How much of Earth's surface does the ocean cover?
What is special about the anglerfish?
opts: It is the largest fish in the sea.

questions.q: According to the text, what does the ocean produce?
opts: More than half of the world's oxygen

questions.q: Why is plastic pollution dangerous for ocean animals?
opts: It makes the water too cold.
It blocks sunlight from reaching them.
Animals can eat it and die.
It makes the water too salty.

questions.title: The Interview
questions.text:
Maria had been unemployed for four months. She had sent out dozens of applications, attended three interviews, and received nothing but polite rejection emails. So when the phone rang on a Thursday morning and a cheerful voice said, "We'd love to invite you for a final interview at Greenfield Publishing," she nearly dropped her coffee mug.

The interview was the following Monday. Maria spent the entire weekend preparing. She researched the company, rehearsed her answers in front of the mirror, and chose her outfit carefully. Her older sister called to wish her luck. "Just be yourself," her sister advised. "They already like you enough to invite you back."

On Monday morning, Maria arrived fifteen minutes early. The receptionist offered her a glass of water and pointed to a set of chairs in the waiting area. Three other candidates were already sitting there, each of them looking calm and confident. Maria felt her heart rate increase.

When her name was called, she walked into a bright office where two people were waiting — a woman named Dr. Ellis and a younger man named James, who turned out to be the head of the editorial team.

The questions were harder than she expected. They asked her to analyse a sample article, suggest improvements, and explain her editorial philosophy. But Maria had prepared well. She answered clearly, asked her own questions, and even made Dr. Ellis laugh with a story about a terrible grammar mistake she once made at university.

As she rode the bus home, she replayed the interview in her mind. She thought she had done well, but she had thought that before. She decided not to overthink it and cooked herself a proper dinner for the first time in weeks.

Four days later, the phone rang again.

questions.q: How long had Maria been looking for a job?
What advice did Maria's sister give her?
What task were candidates asked to do during the interview?
opts: Write a short article on the spot
Analyse a sample article and suggest improvements

questions.q: What does the ending of the story suggest?
opts: Maria decided to give up job hunting.
Maria had another interview the same week.

questions.title: The Psychology of Habits
questions.text:
Every day, we perform hundreds of actions without thinking: brushing our teeth, checking our phone, making coffee, choosing the same route to work. These automatic behaviours are called habits, and they play a far greater role in our lives than most of us realise.

Scientists have discovered that habits form through a process known as the "habit loop." This loop has three parts: a cue, a routine, and a reward. The cue is a signal that triggers the behaviour — it could be a time of day, a location, a feeling, or even a specific person. The routine is the behaviour itself. The reward is what your brain receives at the end, which reinforces the loop and makes it more likely to repeat.

For example, many people feel stressed at work (cue) and walk to the vending machine (routine) to eat a chocolate bar (reward). Over time, this connection becomes automatic. Changing a habit is difficult because the cue and reward remain, but the routine can be replaced. Experts suggest keeping the same cue and reward but substituting a healthier routine — such as taking a short walk instead of visiting the vending machine.

Forming a new habit takes time. A popular belief says it takes 21 days, but research from University College London suggests the real average is closer to 66 days — and the range varies widely from person to person, from 18 days to over 250 days.

Understanding the habit loop can also help us design better environments. If you want to read more, leave a book on your pillow. If you want to eat less junk food, don't buy it. The easier a behaviour is to start, the more likely it is to become a habit.

Our habits are not our destiny, but they shape it more than we think.

questions.q: What are the three parts of the 'habit loop'?
According to the text, how can you change a bad habit effectively?
opts: Completely remove the cue and reward
Replace the routine while keeping the cue and reward the same
Force yourself to stop the behaviour immediately
Avoid all situations that trigger the habit

questions.q: What does the UCL research say about forming a new habit?
opts: It takes an average of 66 days, but varies a lot.
It is impossible after the age of 30.

questions.q: What does the author suggest about environment design?
opts: You should avoid making any changes to your surroundings.
Making a behaviour easy to start helps it become a habit.
Habits only form in familiar environments.
The best habits are formed at work, not at home.

questions.title: Last Train to Kingsbridge
questions.text:
The departure board said 22:47. Daniel checked his watch: 22:44. He had three minutes. He ran.

The station at Waterloo was unusually quiet for a Friday night. His footsteps echoed across the marble floor as he rushed past the coffee kiosk and the shuttered newspaper stand. Platform 12. He could see the green light at the end of the tunnel, but the doors of the last carriage were already beginning to close.

"Wait!" he shouted, though he knew no one was listening.

The doors shut. The train moved. Daniel stood at the edge of the platform, slightly out of breath, watching the red tail lights disappear into the darkness.

He sat down on a metal bench and stared at his phone. There were no more trains to Kingsbridge that night. The next one was at 06:15 in the morning. He had seven and a half hours.

He considered his options. A taxi would cost somewhere between seventy and ninety pounds — money he didn't really have. He could call his friend Amir, but Amir lived in the opposite direction and had work at six. He could find a cheap hotel, but he'd need to be up at five.

Or he could just wait.

He bought a coffee from a vending machine — it tasted like warm cardboard — and settled into the bench with his jacket pulled tight. A cleaner with a mop passed by without looking at him. An elderly woman sat down nearby, placed a neat handbag on her lap, and opened a thick novel without saying a word.

By midnight, Daniel had written three unanswered text messages, read an entire Wikipedia article on the history of locomotive engineering, and decided that missing the last train was, in some strange way, the most peaceful thing that had happened to him all week.

questions.q: Why did Daniel miss the train?
opts: He was on the wrong platform.
He arrived at 22:47, one minute too late.
He arrived at 22:44 but the doors had already closed.
He stopped to buy a coffee.

questions.q: Why didn't Daniel take a taxi?
opts: It was too expensive for him.
He was afraid of taxis at night.

questions.q: What did the coffee from the vending machine taste like?
How did Daniel feel about missing the train by the end of the story?

questions.title: Why Cities Are Getting Hotter
questions.titleTr: Şehirler Neden Giderek Daha Sıcak Oluyor
questions.text:
If you have ever noticed that cities feel significantly hotter than the surrounding countryside, you are not imagining it. This phenomenon has a name: the Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect.

The causes are well understood. Cities are full of materials — concrete, asphalt, and glass — that absorb heat during the day and release it slowly at night. In contrast, natural landscapes such as forests and fields reflect more sunlight and release water vapour through plants, which cools the surrounding air. Cities also generate enormous amounts of heat through cars, air conditioners, and industry.

The consequences can be serious. During heatwaves, urban temperatures can be 5 to 10 degrees Celsius higher than in nearby rural areas. This increases the risk of heat-related illness and death, particularly for elderly people and those who cannot afford air conditioning. Energy demand also spikes, putting pressure on electricity grids.

The good news is that there are practical solutions. Urban planners are increasingly designing cities with more green spaces — parks, urban forests, and green rooftops. Trees provide shade, reduce temperatures, and improve air quality. Some cities, such as Singapore and Melbourne, have set ambitious targets for tree planting in recent years.

Another approach is the use of cool pavements and cool roofs, which are treated with special materials that reflect sunlight rather than absorbing it. These materials can reduce surface temperatures by up to 30 degrees Celsius in direct sunlight.

As climate change continues to push global temperatures higher, finding ways to cool our cities is becoming an urgent priority. The solutions exist; the challenge is implementing them at scale.

questions.q: What is the main cause of the Urban Heat Island effect?
opts: Too many people living in cities
Materials like concrete and asphalt that absorb and store heat
More sunlight reaching cities due to fewer clouds
Global warming affecting only urban areas

questions.q: Who is most at risk during urban heatwaves, according to the text?
opts: Elderly people and those without air conditioning

questions.q: How do cool pavements and cool roofs help?
opts: They absorb more heat and release it underground.
They reflect sunlight instead of absorbing it.
They produce cold air through chemical reactions.
They create shade for people below.

questions.q: What does the author suggest is the main challenge in solving the UHI problem?
opts: Educating the public about the problem

questions.title: The Paradox of Choice
questions.text:
Walk into any modern supermarket and you will find, on average, over 40,000 different products. Dating apps offer thousands of potential partners. Streaming platforms host millions of films and series. Career counsellors advise young people to "follow their passion" — implying that somewhere among the vast landscape of human endeavour, there is a perfect vocation waiting to be discovered. The assumption underlying all of this is simple: more choice is better.

Psychologist Barry Schwartz, in his influential book The Paradox of Choice, argues the opposite. He contends that an abundance of options, far from liberating us, actually increases anxiety, reduces satisfaction, and frequently leads to worse decisions.

His central evidence comes from a now-famous jam study conducted by psychologists Iyengar and Lepper. When a supermarket display offered 24 varieties of jam, it attracted more browsers than a display with only six options. But the smaller display led to ten times as many purchases. Faced with too many options, people experienced what researchers call "choice overload" — a kind of cognitive paralysis that makes it easier to make no decision at all.

Even when we do make a choice from a large set, we tend to be less satisfied. This is partly due to what Schwartz calls the "opportunity cost" of choosing: the more alternatives there are, the more appealing the rejected options seem in retrospect. A person who chose one of three holiday destinations might be perfectly happy. The same person choosing from thirty destinations is more likely to wonder whether they made the right call.

There is also the role of rising expectations. When options are plentiful, we expect to find something perfect. If our chosen option falls short of perfect — as nearly everything does — we attribute the failure to ourselves rather than to the inherent limitations of reality. In a world with only three cereals, a mediocre breakfast is just bad luck. In a world with three hundred cereals, it feels like a personal failure.

The implications extend well beyond shopping. The proliferation of choice in careers, relationships, and lifestyles may be contributing to rising levels of anxiety and dissatisfaction in affluent societies — not despite their abundance, but because of it.

The solution, Schwartz suggests, is not to eliminate choice but to become what he calls a "satisficer" rather than a "maximiser." A satisficer sets reasonable criteria and picks the first option that meets them. A maximiser searches exhaustively for the absolute best. Paradoxically, the satisficer tends to end up happier.

questions.q: What was the key finding of the jam study?
opts: People prefer expensive jams over cheap ones.
More variety always leads to more purchases.
A smaller selection led to far more actual purchases.
Shoppers spent more time choosing when there were fewer options.

questions.q: Why do people feel less satisfied when choosing from a large set of options?
opts: They choose too quickly and regret it.
Rejected options seem more appealing in retrospect.
Large sets usually contain lower-quality items.
They feel pressure from other people's opinions.

questions.q: According to Schwartz, what happens when expectations are high and the chosen option is imperfect?
opts: People try a different option immediately.
People attribute the failure to themselves.

questions.q: What is the difference between a 'satisficer' and a 'maximiser'?
opts: A satisficer spends more money; a maximiser saves money.
A satisficer picks the first option that meets their criteria; a maximiser seeks the absolute best.
A satisficer is indecisive; a maximiser is confident.
A satisficer prefers fewer choices; a maximiser prefers many.

questions.title: The Cartographer's Daughter
questions.text:
My father drew maps for a living, and I grew up believing that the world was something you could reduce to lines. He would spread enormous sheets of paper across the kitchen table — the table where we ate dinner, where my mother paid bills, where I did my homework — and with a steadiness of hand I have never inherited, trace coastlines, mark elevations, shade political boundaries in soft pencil.

"Maps are honest," he told me once, when I was perhaps nine. "They show you exactly what is there." I nodded, because I believed him then.

I was twenty-three when I went to work for a humanitarian organisation in a country that had recently emerged from a long and complicated civil war. My job, ironically, was to update the maps — to correct the records that showed roads where there were now none, towns that had been renamed, borders that had shifted or hardened in ways that official cartography had not yet acknowledged.

What I found was that maps were not honest. Or rather, they were honest about whoever had drawn them. The colonial-era maps showed a landscape carved into geometric territories that paid no attention to the people who actually lived there — their kinship networks, their seasonal migration routes, their sacred sites. Later maps, drawn by post-independence governments, imposed new national narratives onto the same physical terrain. Neither version told the full story.

I spent eighteen months in that country. I learned some of the language. I learned, slowly, that the landscape held layers of meaning that no flat projection could contain — that a single hillside might be simultaneously a military stronghold, a grazing pasture, an ancestral burial ground, and a source of a particular medicinal plant used only by women in one specific village.

When I came home, my father asked what I had learned. I told him that maps were honest, but only partially. He was quiet for a moment, then nodded slowly. "That's what I always believed," he said. "I just never knew how to put it into words."

questions.q: What was the narrator's father's view of maps when the narrator was a child?
opts: He thought maps were political tools.
He believed maps were completely honest.
He saw maps as works of art.
He distrusted maps made by governments.

questions.q: What did the narrator discover about the colonial-era maps?
opts: They were highly accurate but outdated.
They reflected the perspective of those who drew them, ignoring local realities.
They showed more detail than modern maps.
They were drawn by local communities.

questions.q: What does the example of the hillside illustrate?
opts: That maps cannot show enough geographic detail.
That landscapes hold multiple, layered meanings that maps cannot capture.
That military use of land is always hidden from maps.
That local languages are essential for understanding maps.

questions.q: How does the narrator's view of maps change by the end of the story?
opts: She completely rejects her father's view.
She concludes that maps are useless.
She reaches a more nuanced view: maps are honest but only partially.
She decides to become a cartographer like her father.

questions.title: Rewilding: Letting Nature Reclaim Itself
questions.titleTr: Doğaya Geri Dönüş: Doğanın Kendini Yeniden Keşfetmesi
questions.text:
For most of human history, our relationship with the natural world has been defined by control — clearing forests, draining wetlands, erecting fences, and systematically removing predators that threatened livestock. The result is a world that is, in ecological terms, significantly impoverished. In Europe alone, it is estimated that 70% of animal populations have declined since 1970.

Rewilding is a conservation philosophy that inverts this logic. Rather than managing nature intensively, it proposes allowing — and in some cases actively facilitating — natural processes to restore themselves. Its most dramatic expression involves reintroducing apex predators to ecosystems from which they were eliminated.

The most celebrated example comes from Yellowstone National Park in the United States. When wolves were reintroduced in 1995 after a 70-year absence, the effects were far more complex than anyone anticipated. The wolves reduced the elk population, which had been overgrazing riverbanks. With the pressure lifted, riverbank vegetation began to recover. The recovering vegetation stabilised the river channels, reduced erosion, and created habitat for beavers, birds, and fish. This chain of indirect ecological effects — in which a top predator restructures an entire ecosystem — is known as a "trophic cascade."

In Europe, rewilding projects have attempted to reintroduce beavers, lynx, bison, and, in a few cases, wolves. The results have been broadly positive for biodiversity, though the projects have not been without controversy. Farmers in the vicinity of wolf and lynx territories report livestock losses and express concerns about personal safety — concerns that, while sometimes statistically overstated, are understandable given the cultural and economic stakes involved.

Rewilding also raises uncomfortable questions about human agency. Who decides which species belong in a given ecosystem? How should we balance the interests of rural communities against the interests of biodiversity? And is it even possible to return to a "natural" state in a world shaped by thousands of years of human activity?

Proponents argue that these questions, while legitimate, should not paralyse action. The biodiversity crisis is urgent. Rewilding does not require perfection; it requires the humility to acknowledge that in many cases, nature — given the space and time — can do a better job of managing itself than we can.

words.tr: tepe yırtıcısı, zincirin en üstündeki yırtıcı

questions.q: What does the Yellowstone wolf reintroduction demonstrate?
opts: That apex predators are dangerous and should be controlled.
That predator reintroduction only affects prey populations.
That top predators can restructure entire ecosystems through indirect effects.
That rewilding is only possible in large national parks.

questions.q: Why do some farmers oppose rewilding projects involving wolves and lynx?
opts: They believe it will increase tourism and disrupt their land.
They report livestock losses and safety concerns.
They disagree with the scientific evidence for trophic cascades.
They prefer government subsidies to ecological restoration.

questions.q: What ethical dilemma does rewilding raise, according to the article?
opts: Whether animals have rights equal to humans.
Who decides which species belong in an ecosystem and how to balance competing interests.
Whether it is morally acceptable to remove humans from rural areas.
How to compensate farmers for land used in rewilding projects.

questions.q: What is the author's overall position on rewilding?
opts: Strongly opposed due to the risks to farming communities.
Neutral — presenting both sides without a clear stance.
Broadly supportive, arguing that the urgency of biodiversity loss justifies action despite imperfections.
Critical of rewilding as an impractical idealistic concept.

questions.title: What the Silence Contained
questions.text:
They had not spoken properly in three years. Not about anything that mattered.

Every Sunday, Clara drove to her mother's house in the village — forty minutes on the motorway and then ten along a road that curved through fields. She brought bread, sometimes flowers, occasionally a bottle of wine her mother would not drink. Her mother made tea. They sat at the kitchen table and talked about the weather, about Clara's colleague who was getting married, about the leak in the bathroom that had been repaired six months ago and was apparently still worth mentioning.

The silence around the real things — her father's death, the money dispute, the things said in grief that neither of them had taken back — was not hostile. It was, Clara had decided, structural. It held the rest of the conversation up, the way a wall holds up a roof. Remove it, and everything collapses.

On the Sunday after Clara's fortieth birthday, she arrived to find her mother in the garden, standing very still at the edge of the vegetable patch, staring at nothing in particular. Clara watched her from the gate for a moment before her mother turned.

"There was a heron here this morning," her mother said. "Stood there for nearly an hour."

Clara walked over and stood beside her, looking at the empty space where the heron had been. They stood in silence for a while. Then her mother said, without looking at her: "I know I wasn't fair to you. After your father died. I know that."

Clara did not answer immediately. She looked at the turned earth, the staked tomatoes, the careful order her mother maintained against the wildness that pressed in at the edges.

"I know," she said, finally. "I wasn't either."

They stood a little longer. Then they went inside for tea, and talked about the heron, and for the first time in three years the silence between the real things felt slightly different — not smaller, but less heavy. As if something, without being named, had shifted.

questions.q: What does Clara bring to her mother's house every Sunday?
How does Clara describe the silence around the difficult subjects?
opts: Structural — something that holds the rest of the conversation up
Temporary and soon to be resolved

questions.q: What causes a shift in their relationship on Clara's fortieth birthday visit?
opts: Clara finally confronts her mother directly.
Her mother spontaneously acknowledges that she wasn't fair to Clara.
Clara decides to forgive her mother silently.

questions.q: At the end of the story, how has the silence between them changed?
opts: It feels less heavy, though it is not smaller.
It has become more hostile than before.
It is exactly the same as always.
